approach: the CR answers a MEANING question the codebase could not answer for itself, and the answer is a code change with no behavior in it. Issue #20 (CR 10's own followup, seq 6) asks whether tmux's !window guards on from and size are dead code or deliberate intent. Blast is low and no hard floor is reachable: no spec edit, no suite edit, argv byte-identical, so no narrowing to clear, no semver class to exceed, and nothing for a Conflict to contradict. auto-all rather than auto-spec because there is no spec gate to reach — the frozen contract already covers this behavior completely and stays untouched.

the decision is DELETE the guards, by lexically scoping from and size into the pane-split branch. It is not a coin flip between two defensible options: scoping strictly DOMINATES the guard on the axis the issue cares about. A guard only acts while the value is in scope, so it neutralizes a leak silently; scoping removes the value from the window branch's scope, so the same mistake stops compiling. Established by probe rather than argument — wiring ...size/...from into the window branch on origin/main COMPILES and passes all 510 tests (the guard empties it), while the same edit on the new code is TS2304 Cannot find name. The guard was not defending against the leak, it was concealing it, which is precisely why the natural reading the issue complains about is wrong.

herdr settles WHICH answer without appeal to taste. session.herdr.ts already scopes from and size inside its split branch, and the frozen 'from is ignored by tab and workspace' scenario covers herdr as well as tmux — so the seam contract already accepts structure-alone as satisfying it, and has since before this CR. CR 10's own spec-judge recorded the asymmetry as an observation (seq 2): the ratio-is-a-split-concept scenario is tmux-only BECAUSE herdr's size is lexically scoped and cannot leak. tmux was the adapter that had not caught up, not a second legitimate house style.

no changeset: the package is published but nothing user-facing moves — argv is byte-identical across the whole option matrix, proven differentially against origin/main's adapter rather than inferred from a green suite, and the differential was itself mutation-tested to confirm it can fail.

the two split-window ternary arms collapse into one branch with a direction variable. Not scope creep but a consequence — a ternary arm cannot hold a const, so scoping the values requires the branch — and it lands on the shape herdr already uses, removing the duplicated argv spelling that let the two arms drift.

the stale test comment is corrected rather than left standing, the same class of defect CR 10's impl-judge caught in its own record. session.tmux.test.ts documented the guard as belt-and-braces and named the wrong subject as an author who wires ...size into the window branch; after this change that subject does not compile, so the comment would have overclaimed what the test can catch. The honest remaining subject is an author who hoists the value back out to function scope AND spreads it in — a backstop against the structure being dismantled, not against a flag being passed.

why: cold sdd-impl-judge (fresh context, independent) re-derived every oracle from source rather than from this CR's prose: IMPLEMENTATION_PASS true, zero failing, no blocker, no absorption findings, no content gaps. pnpm verify 6/6, 510 tests. No spec gate ran and none was owed — the frozen contract already specified this behavior completely, so the CR carries no suite diff (the judge confirmed git diff HEAD over mux.feature and spec.md is EMPTY and status stays implemented), which is also why the leash is auto-all rather than auto-spec. The judge attacked the two claims that actually carry this CR rather than re-reading the diff. FIRST, byte-identical argv: it extracted HEAD's adapter and ran its OWN differential matrix — 320 combinations of at x from x ratio x env x label, both adapters, first exec call diffed — 0 mismatches across flag order, direction, -t, -l and the -e pairs. That is an independent instrument, not a re-run of the producer's; the producer's own matrix was separately mutation-tested (direction flipped) to prove it could fail at all. SECOND, and the finding that decides the issue: it verified the compile-error claim in BOTH directions by mutation. On the new structure, wiring ...from/...size into the window branch is TS2304 Cannot find name. On HEAD's guard structure the IDENTICAL edit compiles clean AND passes 71/71 silently, because the guard empties the value whatever is spread in. So the guard never defended against the leak — it concealed it, which is exactly why the reading the issue calls natural (that the guard is what prevents the leak) is wrong, and why this is not a choice between two defensible options: scoping strictly dominates. THIRD, it caught the one thing this CR asserted without proving — that the rewritten test comment does not overclaim. It reproduced the compound wrong subject the comment names (hoist size back to function scope AND spread it into the window branch, which is legal TS) and confirmed the ratio-is-a-split-concept scenario then FAILS red. The scenario is not inert and the comment is honest about what it can and can no longer catch. Structural read: the change reduces duplication and improves fit — collapsing the two ternary arms into if/else with a direction variable removes an argv spelling that was written twice and could drift, and lands on the shape session.herdr.ts already uses. Judged correct to leave the .feature untouched: no scenario's observable meaning changed, only how hard the same contract is to violate, so no narrowing, no re-open, no floor. Self-asserted within the auto-all leash (by derived) — ratify or kick back.
